questions.text: My emotional responses have a kinetic quality — they propel me to move, speak, react before I've decided to.
I am drawn to the captain's chair, not the crew's quarters.
I avoid ultimatums and prefer to keep negotiating until everyone is on board.
I hold the view that some of the most important discoveries come from wandering without a clear plan.
I hold the view that the simplest interpretation of someone's words is usually the correct one.
When I feel at peace, the sensation is so vivid and complete that it's almost sacred.
I am drawn to situations where every voice carries equal weight.
I rarely notice when the formality of an interaction is mismatched with the actual closeness of the relationship.
I treat small daily choices as building blocks for long-term outcomes.
I notice that when I feel passionate about something, it becomes all-consuming rather than a background interest.
I find that I am able to make hard choices about resource allocation without being influenced by the emotional intensity of individual cases.
I find that I am usually the one setting the standards for a group.
I make it a point to arrive early to appointments and meetings.
I am highly attuned to when an apology is genuine versus perfunctory.
I confirm details and expectations before starting any collaborative task.
I notice when someone changes the subject to avoid an uncomfortable topic.
I feel that my own creative output is the most important thing I produce.
I feel a strong pull to put community needs above my personal desires.
I tend to miss when someone uses a particular anecdote to send a message to someone else in the group.
I find it hard to stay quiet when I disagree with the group's direction.
When there is a vacuum of leadership, I look around to see who else might step in.
I believe that the moral weight I assign to others' pain is the foundation on which all of my other ethical commitments rest.
I believe that the less I focus on my own needs, the more I can focus on what really matters.
I hold the view that the moral concern generated by suffering is the inner compass that most reliably points me toward the right action in ambiguous circumstances.
Planning and execution blend together for me; I think and do simultaneously.
I notice the micro-adjustments people make to their clothing or hair when someone they want to impress is nearby.
I jump into the deep end with new skills and piece together understanding through experience.
I hold the view that remaining rationally centered during emotional upheaval is a capacity that supports not only my own well-being but the well-being of everyone I work alongside.
I would spend my sabbatical working on behalf of a community project rather than resting.
I can engage with intense subject matter — war, loss, tragedy — without being emotionally flooded.
I find that stressful situations don't noticeably change my heart rate or breathing.
I find that prioritizing my own goals gives me the energy and clarity I need to thrive.
I notice that my body doesn't tend to broadcast my emotional state — my face and posture stay neutral.
I think that the ability to engage with suffering without emotional destabilization is a form of resilience that serves both the helper and the helped.
I feel that self-investment is the most reliable path to long-term fulfillment.
I hold the view that treating another's pain as genuinely important is not a sentiment but an action that requires discipline.
